title: relay-panel vs Alternatives: Which Backend Tool Should You Choose?
description: Compare relay-panel with popular Backend alternatives. See features, performance, and community stats.

sourceData.name: relay-panel
sourceData.description: Self-hosted TCP/UDP forwarding management panel with relay-node, WebSocket control, quotas, and traffic stats.

bodyHtml:
relay-panel is making waves in the Backend ecosystem this week, gaining 0 new stars. With a total of 32 stars on GitHub and 8 forks, this open-source project is rapidly becoming a go-to tool for developers worldwide.

⭐ 32Stars
📈 +0/wkGrowth
🔀 8Forks
⚠️ 0Open Issues

Key Features

  • Written in Rust for maximum performance
  • Licensed under AGPL-3.0 — free to use and modify
  • Official website: https://relaypanel.dev
  • 🏷️ panel
  • 🏷️ relay
  • 🏷️ rust
  • 🏷️ self-hosted
  • 🏷️ tcp-forwarding

Why relay-panel Is Trending

The Backend landscape is constantly evolving, and relay-panel has emerged as one of the fastest-growing projects this week. Several factors contribute to its momentum:

  1. Growing community: With 0 new stars this week alone, developer adoption is accelerating rapidly.
  2. Active maintenance: The project sees regular updates, ensuring compatibility with the latest ecosystem changes.
  3. Practical utility: Self-hosted TCP/UDP forwarding management panel with relay-node, WebSocket control, quotas, and traffic stats.

If you're working in Backend, this is definitely a project worth watching — and adopting.

How It Compares to Alternatives

When evaluating relay-panel, it's natural to compare it against established alternatives. Here's what sets it apart:

  • Performance: Built with Rust, it leverages modern language features for speed.
  • Community size: 32 stars indicate strong community trust and validation.
  • License: AGPL-3.0 licensing means no commercial restrictions.

Final Thoughts

relay-panel is a solid addition to any developer's toolkit. With its growing community, active development, and practical design, it's well worth your time to evaluate. Star it on GitHub, try it in a side project, and see if it fits your workflow.
